attributes.stem:
A 67-year-old man is day 3 post an elective right total knee replacement for severe osteoarthritis. He initially made a good post-operative recovery and was able to mobilise with minimal pain. However, he has now become confused, voluble and difficult to control. He is not responding to requests from the nursing staff who have attempted verbal de-escalation and is making threatening comments.

He has a medical history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes. His regular medications include atenolol and metformin. He has a 30 pack-year smoking history and consumes four glasses of wine daily.

On examination, he appears agitated and is sitting on the edge of the bed, arguing with the nurses. Further attempts at verbal de-escalation are unsuccessful. His vital signs from two hours ago show a blood pressure of 100/70 mmHg, pulse rate 100/min and temperature 38.7°C. Further examination is not possible as he is uncooperative and becomes physically aggressive when approached.

Blood tests from this morning were unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Repeat vital sign observations in 1 hour
attributes.explanation:
There are many reasons why this patient might be confused, and all options are important to consider; however, considering his current state of heightened anxiety, none of these approaches are likely to meet with much success until he has calmed down - or been given something to help calm him down. The nursing staff and medical officer have already attempted verbal de-escalation; thus, an olanzapine wafer is the most important first step before attempting to further investigate the cause of his confusion. Olanzapine, an atypical antipsychotic, can help calm the patient and reduce agitation, allowing for further assessment, examination and management of this patient. Obviously if the patient adamantly refuses to take the olanzapine wafer, then other methods of restraint may need to be considered.

Repeating vital sign observations in 1 hour is not the most appropriate next step, given new fever and borderline tachycardia may represent an emergent infection or sepsis. While regular monitoring is important, the patient's current aggressive state makes it unsafe to attempt observations. Moreover, waiting an hour could potentially allow the situation to escalate further.

Administering paracetamol could be beneficial given the patient's fever, but it's not the most urgent intervention. The patient's agitation needs to be addressed first to ensure safety and allow for further assessment.

A full septic screen is an important consideration given the patient's fever and recent surgery. However, in the patient's current agitated state, it would be difficult and potentially dangerous to attempt blood cultures, urine tests, or other investigations. This should be done as soon as practically possible and when safe to do so.

Examination of the right knee is also important to rule out post-operative complications, but again, this cannot be safely or effectively done while the patient is combative.

After administering olanzapine and once the patient is calmer, a thorough assessment should be conducted. This would include repeating vital signs, performing a full physical examination (including the operative site), and conducting appropriate investigations such as a septic screen, given the fever. It's crucial to identify and treat the underlying cause of the delirium, which could include infection, pain, electrolyte imbalances, or withdrawal from alcohol, among other possibilities. The management of delirium should involve a multimodal approach, addressing both the immediate behavioural disturbance and the underlying precipitating factors.
learningPoints: Attempting to engage and treat a patient when they are verbally or physically aggressive or combative is not advisable prior to de-escalation.
Managing delirium requires a multimodal approach to identify and treat underlying precipitating cause of confusion, as well as preventing further episodes
Surgery is a common cause of delirium, but need to consider alternative acute causes such as sepsis, hypoglycaemia, dehydration and pain.
references: Patel MX, Sethi FN, Barnes TR, Dix R, Dratcu L, Fox B, Garriga M, Haste JC, Kahl KG, Lingford-Hughes A, McAllister-Williams H, O'Brien A, Parker C, Paterson B, Paton C, Posporelis S, Taylor DM, Vieta E, Völlm B, Wilson-Jones C, Woods L; With co-authors (in alphabetical order):. Joint BAP NAPICU evidence-based consensus guidelines for the clinical management of acute disturbance: De-escalation and rapid tranquillisation. J Psychopharmacol. 2018 Jun;32(6):601-640. doi: 10.1177/0269881118776738. Epub 2018 Jun 8. PMID: 29882463.
Wilson, J.E., Mart, M.F., Cunningham, C. et al. Delirium. Nat Rev Dis Primers 6, 90 (2020). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41572-020-00223-4
